title: Bartleby the Scrivener A Story of Wall Street
author: Herman Melville
prompt:
Question: Which of the following passages is verbatim from the "Bartleby the Scrivener A Story of Wall Street" book by Herman Melville?
Options:
A. The clerks worked in shifts, relieving each other periodically. Nippers worked while Turkey was off, and vice versa. This setup worked well under the circumstances. Ginger Nut, the third clerk, was a twelve-year-old boy. His father, a cart driver, hoped to see his son become a lawyer rather than follow his own career path before passing away. So the father sent the boy to my office to study law, run errands, clean, and do odd jobs for one dollar per week. He had a small personal desk but hardly used it. When I inspected the drawer, it was filled with many empty nutshells.
B. The clerks worked in shifts, substituting for one another periodically. Nippers worked while Turkey was off duty, and vice versa. This setup was sensible given the circumstances. Ginger Nut, the third clerk, was a boy of around twelve years old. His father, a cart driver, hoped to see his son become a lawyer rather than follow in his footsteps. So the father sent the boy to my office to study law, run errands, and clean in exchange for one dollar per week. The boy had his own little desk but he rarely used it. The desk drawer contained many discarded nutshells when examined.
C. Their fits relieved each other like guards. When Nippers was on, Turkeys was off; and vice versa. This was a good natural arrangement under the circumstances. Ginger Nut, the third on my list, was a lad some twelve years old. His father was a carman, ambitious of seeing his son on the bench instead of a cart, before he died. So he sent him to my office as student at law, errand boy, and cleaner and sweeper, at the rate of one dollar a week. He had a little desk to himself, but he did not use it much. Upon inspection, the drawer exhibited a great array of the shells of various sorts of nuts.
D. The clerks worked in shifts, taking turns being on duty. When Nippers was working, Turkey was off, and the other way around. This arrangement made sense under the circumstances. Ginger Nut, the third clerk on my list, was a young boy of about twelve years old. His father worked as a cart driver and dreamed of his son becoming a lawyer instead of a cart driver like himself before he died. So he sent the boy to my office to learn about the law, deliver messages, clean, and do odd jobs for one dollar per week. He had his own small desk but rarely used it. The desk drawer was filled with all kinds of discarded nutshells when I looked inside.
Answer:

Ginger Nut, the third clerk, was a twelve-year-old boy. His father, a cart driver, hoped to see his son become a lawyer rather than follow his own career path before passing away. So the father sent the boy to my office to study law, run errands, clean, and do odd jobs for one dollar per week. He had a small personal desk but hardly used it. When I inspected the drawer, it was filled with many empty nutshells.

The desk drawer contained many discarded nutshells when examined.
